perturbation.original_text: 4.3 The Recipient irrevocably and unconditionally agrees and undertakes 4.3.1 to treat and safeguard the Confidential Information as strictly private, secret and confidential; 4.3.2 not to use or permit the use of the Confidential Information for any purpose other than for the Permitted Purpose and, in particular, not to use or permit the use of the Confidential Information, whether directly or indir ectly, to obtain a commercial, trading, investment, financial or other advantage over the Disclosing Party and/or its Associates or otherwise use it to the detriment of the Disclosing Party and/or its Associates;
perturbation.changed_text: 4.3 The Recipient irrevocably and unconditionally agrees and undertakes 4.3.1 to treat and safeguard the Confidential Information as strictly private, secret and confidential; 4.3.2 not to use or permit the use of the Confidential Information for any purpose other than for the Permitted Purpose and, in particular, may use the Confidential Information, whether directly or indirectly, to obtain a commercial, trading, investment, financial or other advantage over the Disclosing Party and/or its Associates or otherwise use it to the detriment of the Disclosing Party and/or its Associates, provided the Disclosing party is notify in writing.
perturbation.explanation: This change alters the 'not to use' clause to 'may use' with notification. This directly contradicts the confidentiality undertakings specified in Clause 4.3.1 and overall purpose of the agreement, where the core tenet is the protection of confidential information. This creates a fundamental in-text contradiction to safeguard confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.3 The Recipient irrevocably and unconditionally agrees and undertakes 4.3.1 to treat and safeguard the Confidential Information as strictly private, secret and confidential; 4.3.2 not to use or permit the use of the Confidential Information for any purpose other than for the Permitted Purpose and, in particular, not to use or permit the use of the Confidential Information, whether directly or indir ectly, to obtain a commercial, trading, investment, financial or other advantage over the Disclosing Party and/or its Associates or otherwise use it to the detriment of the Disclosing Party and/or its Associates; 4.3.3 not to disclose the Confidential Informati on to any third party for any reason or purpose whatsoever without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, save in accordance with the provisions of this Agreement; 4.3.4 not to decompile, disassemble or reverse engineer or otherwise modify, adapt, a lter or vary the whole or any part of the Confidential Information; 4.3.5 not to copy or reproduce the Confidential Information by any means without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, it being TE NDA MUTUAL CONFIDENTIALITY AND NON -DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT 31 recorded that any copies shall be and remain the prop erty of the Disclosing Party; and 4.3.6 to keep all Confidential Information safely and securely and to take all such steps as may be reasonably necessary to protect it against theft, damage, loss, unauthorised access (including access by electronic means) and t o prevent Confidential Information from falling into the hands of unauthorised third parties.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies clause 4.3.2 from 'not to use' to 'may use' the confidential information for commercial advantage with notification. This directly contradicts the core principle of confidentiality and the undertaking in 4.3.1 to treat the information as strictly private, secret, and confidential. The contradicted text, specifically 4.3.1, explicitly requires safeguarding the confidentiality of the information, which is undermined by allowing its use for commercial gain, even with notification. This aligns with the definition of 'Structural Flaws - In Text Contradiction', where conflicting implications are introduced.

perturbation.original_text: 13.1 The Disclosing Party may, at any time, request the Receiving Party to return any material containing, pertaining to or relating to Confidential Information and may, in addition request the Receiving Party to furnish a written statement to the effect that, upon such return, the Receiving Party has not retained in its possession, or under its control, either directly or indirectly, any such material.
perturbation.changed_text: 13.1 The Disclosing Party may, at any time, request the Receiving Party to return any material containing, pertaining to or relating to Confidential Information and may, in addition request the Receiving Party to furnish a written statement to the effect that, upon such return, the Receiving Party has retained in its possession, or under its control, either directly or indirectly, any such material.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'has not retained' to 'has retained,' this perturbation creates a direct contradiction. The original clause intends the Receiving Party to confirm they've returned all confidential information and have nothing left. The modification compels them to confirm they *still* possess it. This creates an in-text contradiction making the action that recipient have to do, conflicting.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 13.1 The Disclosing Party may, at any time, request the Receiving Party to return any material containing, pertaining to or relating to Confidential Information and may, in addition request the Receiving Party to furnish a written statement to the effect that, upon such return, the Receiving Party has retained in its possession, or under its control, either directly or indirectly, any such material.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies section 13.1 to state that the Receiving Party confirms they *have retained* confidential information after being requested to return it, which directly contradicts the original intent and the purpose of the clause, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation. The Contradicted Text is the same as the Changed Text, which further highlights the contradiction caused by the modification of the clause. It now implies that the recipient is required to do the opposite of what was originally intended. This creates a structural flaw causing an in-text contradiction as defined.

perturbation.original_text: 18. BREACH 18.1 Without prejudice to the other rights of the Disclosing Party, in the event of any unauthorised disclosure or use of the Confidential Information which is or is reasonably likely to constitute a breach of any provision of this Agreement, the Recipient shall, at the sole cost of the Recipient – 18.1.1 immediately notify the Disclosing Party in writing and take such steps as the Disclosing Party may reasonably require in order to remedy or mitigate the effects of su ch actual or threatened breach; and; 18.1.2 use all reasonable commercial endeavours to assist the Disclosing Party in recovering and preventing the use, dissemination, sale or other disposal of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 18. BREACH 18.1 Without prejudice to the other rights of the Disclosing Party, in the event of any unauthorised disclosure or use of the Confidential Information which is or is reasonably likely to constitute a breach of any provision of this Agreement, the Recipient may choose to, at the sole cost of the Disclosing Party – 18.1.1 immediately notify the Disclosing Party in writing and take such steps as the Disclosing Party may reasonably require in order to remedy or mitigate the effects of su ch actual or threatened breach; and; 18.1.2 use all reasonable commercial endeavours to assist the Disclosing Party in recovering and preventing the use, dissemination, sale or other disposal of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation modifies the breach clause by changing 'the Recipient shall' to 'the Recipient may choose to' and shifting the cost burden from the Recipient to the Disclosing Party. This contradicts the original intent which mandates the Recipient to take immediate action and bear the costs in case of a breach. Thus, the clause now contradicts itself, creating uncertainty as to whether the Recipient is obligated to act in case of a breach and who should bear the costs.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 18. BREACH 18.1 Without prejudice to the other rights of the Disclosing Party, in the event of any unauthorised disclosure or use of the Confidential Information which is or is reasonably likely to constitute a breach of any provision of this Agreement, the Recipient may choose to, at the sole cost of the Disclosing Party – 18.1.1 immediately notify the Disclosing Party in writing and take such steps as the Disclosing Party may reasonably require in order to remedy or mitigate the effects of su ch actual or threatened breach; and; 18.1.2 use all reasonable commercial endeavours to assist the Disclosing Party in recovering and preventing the use, dissemination, sale or other disposal of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the obligation of the Recipient from 'shall' to 'may choose to', and shifts the cost burden from the Recipient to the Disclosing Party. The Contradicted text is identical to the Changed text, but the original intent was for the Recipient to take immediate action and bear the costs. The modification makes the Recipient's action optional and places the financial burden on the Disclosing party, therefore creating a contradiction about who is responsible for what, which falls under the Structural Flaws definition of in-text contradiction.
